flashcards.front: What is the key advantage of a Multi-tape Turing Machine over a single-tape TM?
flashcards.back: Multi-tape TMs can simulate any single-tape TM and often provide quadratic time speedup. A single-tape TM can simulate a k-tape TM in O(n²) time, making multi-tape TMs more convenient for designing algorithms.

flashcards.front: How does a Multi-tape TM simulate a single-tape TM?
flashcards.back: The simulation uses a single tape with special separator symbols (#) to mark boundaries between the k virtual tapes. The TM must make multiple passes to simulate one step of the original MTM - moving right to find the current positions, then moving left to update symbols.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of simulating a k-tape TM on a single-tape TM?
flashcards.back: O(n²) time. For each simulated step, the single-tape TM must make up to 2n moves to scan all tape contents (finding current head positions), resulting in quadratic slowdown.

An NDTM is a 7-tuple M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject) where δ: Q × Γ → P(Q × Γ × {L,R}) is the transition function mapping to subsets (allowing multiple possible moves). It accepts a string if at least one computation path leads to acceptance.

flashcards.front: How does a DTM simulate an NDTM?
flashcards.back: Using breadth-first search (BFS) on the configuration tree. The DTM explores all possible computation paths systematically, keeping track of configurations on its tape. If any branch reaches accept state, the DTM accepts; if all branches reject, it rejects.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity overhead when simulating an NDTM with d branches on a DTM?
flashcards.back: Exponential overhead: if the NDTM has maximum branching factor d and runs in time T(n), the DTM simulation takes O(d^T(n)) time. This demonstrates P ≠ NP (assuming P ≠ NP).

A UTM is a TM that can simulate any other TM. It takes as input: (1) an encoding of the TM to be simulated, and (2) an encoding of the input string. The UTM interprets the encoding and performs the exact computation of the encoded TM.

flashcards.front: Why is the Universal Turing Machine significant?
flashcards.back: The UTM demonstrates that a single general-purpose computer can perform any computation that any specialized machine can do - the foundation of modern computing. It formalizes the stored-program concept.

An LBA is a restricted TM where the tape head cannot move beyond the portion containing the input. Formally: M = (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q₀, q_accept, q_reject) with tape bounded by the input length. It's a non-deterministic device that accepts context-sensitive languages.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between LBAs and Context-Sensitive Languages?
flashcards.back: LBAs accept exactly the context-sensitive languages (Type-1 in Chomsky hierarchy). The equivalence: LBA = Deterministic LBA (the LSP - Linear Speedup Property holds for LBAs).

The thesis states that any computable function can be computed by a Turing Machine. This means the basic TM and all its extensions (MTM, NDTM, UTM) are computationally equivalent - they all compute the same class of functions.

flashcards.front: Are Multi-tape TMs more powerful than single-tape TMs?
flashcards.back: No. According to the Church-Turing Thesis, they are computationally equivalent. Multi-tape TMs are more convenient for algorithm design but don't increase computational power. Any MTM can be simulated by a single-tape TM.
